summary: Cantrill responds to IBM's criticism that OpenSolaris is a 'facade' by dismissing it as a corporate temper tantrum and offering constructive alternatives. He proposes several ways IBM could contribute to open source, from the legally difficult (open-sourcing OS/2 or AIX) to the culturally challenging (supporting OpenSolaris ports to IBM hardware). His most serious suggestion is for IBM to port DTrace to AIX, which would benefit IBM customers and allow IBM to participate meaningfully in the open source community without giving up any IP.
key_points: IBM accused OpenSolaris of being a 'facade', which Cantrill dismisses as corporate posturing
Legal encumbrances from the 'Bad Old Days' of Unix licensing make open-sourcing AIX or OS/2 impractical
IBM could support OpenSolaris ports to POWER or S/390 without assisting Sun hardware sales
Porting DTrace to AIX would directly benefit IBM customers and is technically feasible as Apple demonstrated
The post frames IBM's criticism as counterproductive compared to constructive open source collaboration
money_quotes: I would liken IBM's latest outburst to something of a temper tantrum – and as with a screaming toddler, the best way to deal with this is to not reward the performance, but rather to offer some constructive alternatives.
AIX is one of the true enterprise-class operating systems – one with a long history of running business-critical applications.
Sun doesn't sell POWER-based gear, so you would have the comfort of knowing that your efforts would in no way assist a Sun hardware sale.
Your customers want it. Apple has shown that it can be done. We'll help you do it.
So to IBM I say what parents have said to screaming toddlers for time immemorial: we're ready when you are.
key_insight: Rather than engaging in corporate mudslinging about open source credentials, companies like IBM would better serve their customers by actually contributing to open source projects like DTrace that deliver tangible technical benefits.
